The problem presents a scenario involving student relocation between two dormitories.
We must determine the initial population of Oakwood based on the final state.
There are two dormitories named Oakwood and Maplewood in this university.
A total of ninety-six students reside in both buildings combined.
Five students moved from Maplewood to Oakwood to equalize the populations.
After this move, both dormitories hold the same number of residents.
Since the total is ninety-six, each dormitory now has forty-eight students.
This means Oakwood currently has forty-eight students after the transfer.
Maplewood also currently has forty-eight students after losing five residents.
We need to calculate the number of students in Oakwood before the move.
Before the relocation, Oakwood had fewer students than it does now.
Specifically, Oakwood gained five students during this specific event.
Therefore, we subtract five from the current count of forty-eight.
The calculation shows that forty-eight minus five equals forty-three.
So Oakwood originally had forty-three students before the relocation occurred.
Maplewood originally had more students than it does at the present time.
Maplewood lost five students to reach its current count of forty-eight.
Adding five back to forty-eight gives us fifty-three for Maplewood.
We can verify that forty-three plus fifty-three equals the total of ninety-six.
The sum of the initial populations matches the given total perfectly.
The difference between the initial populations was exactly ten students.
Five students moved to close this gap of ten between the two groups.
This confirms that the math behind the relocation is sound and correct.
The question asks for the number of students in Oakwood before the move.
That number is definitely forty-three based on our logical deduction.
